optimist.name: Rainy Beckerman
optimist.column:
The Mariners lost yesterday. Again. The Astros handed them a 7-8 defeat, and now they’re 6-9, 1.5 games behind in the AL West. It’s the kind of loss that makes you want to throw your coffee in the trash, but I’m not here for that. I’m here to talk about the blue skies, Seattle.  

Let’s be real: this team isn’t built to win every game. They’re built to *belong* to us. To feel like the kind of team that doesn’t just show up but *radiates* something bigger than a win or loss. Yesterday’s game was a reminder that the road to belonging is paved with setbacks, not shortcuts. The Astros are a good team, sure, but the Mariners didn’t fold. They fought. They swung. They kept the faith. That’s the thing about this roster — they’re not here to chase a .500 record

pessimist.name: Walt Varga
pessimist.column:
Another quiet offense, another wasted start. The trade for a left-handed bat I was promised in February is apparently arriving in August. Of 2031.  

The Astros didn’t just beat us yesterday—they embarrassed us. Not with a blowout, but with the kind of clinical efficiency that makes you wonder if we’re still practicing our swings in the batting cage. Houston’s offense didn’t just outperform ours; it outmaneuvered us. Their lineup didn’t just hit; it hit with purpose, while our own hitters stood there like they were waiting for a weather report. Again. Again. Again.  

This isn’t about the score. It’s about the pattern. The same pattern that’s been unfolding since the trade deadline: a team that can’t string together hits, can’t capitalize on opportunities, and can’t stop the other team
